channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: Mathematicians solve decades-old mystery about the hidden order in high-dimensional randomness - Phys.org
item.description: Mathematicians solve decades-old mystery about the hidden order in high-dimensional randomness  Phys.org

item.description:
1. NASA reorganizes to accelerate Moon Base, lunar programs  Ground News

  3. NASA unveils sweeping reorganization  SpaceNews
  4. NASA reorganization shuffle combines programs, lands KSC new director  Orlando Sentinel
  5. Donald Trump reorganises NASA to beat Xi Jinping in the lunar race between them  Atalayar

item.title: Stephen Hawking’s father feared for ‘lazy’ son, diary reveals - The Times
item.description: Stephen Hawking’s father feared for ‘lazy’ son, diary reveals  The TimesSee more headlines & perspectives on Google News

item.title: The Milky Way ate another galaxy. Scientists say they've found the scraps - KSL.com
item.description:
1. The Milky Way ate another galaxy. Scientists say they've found the scraps  KSL.com
  2. The Milky Way ate another galaxy. Scientists say they’ve found the scraps  CNN
  3. Study: Strange stars may actually be remnants of a galaxy the Milky Way 'ate'  WKRC
  4. Did Milky Way secretly swallow another galaxy? Ancient stars hint at lost star system  WION
  5. Scientists think they may have found the remains of an entire galaxy that the Milky Way absorbed billions of years ago — 20 unusual stars hiding inside our own galactic disk, possibly the leftovers of a small galaxy our own home swallowed when it was still you  Space Daily

item.title: Webb just clocked nearly 9,000 young star clusters and found the biggest ones break from their birth clouds in 5 million years, a timing clue that could reshape how astronomers model galaxies growing up - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Webb just clocked nearly 9,000 young star clusters and found the biggest ones break from their birth clouds in 5 million years, a timing clue that could reshape how astronomers model galaxies growing up  Space Daily
  2. Webb Studies Star Clusters  NASA (.gov)
  3. CNS Astronomer Daniela Calzetti Looks into the Cradle Where Stars are Born : College of Natural Sciences  UMass Amherst
  4. Webb Reveals M51’s Rapid Star Cluster Emergence in the Whirlpool Galaxy  TechEBlog -
  5. NASA's James Webb Space Telescope Reveals Massive Star Clusters Form Faster Inside Galaxies  NDTV

item.title: China merges space programmes as race to the moon with US heats up - South China Morning Post
item.description:
1. China merges space programmes as race to the moon with US heats up  South China Morning Post
  2. Isaacman expects Chinese crewed mission around the moon in 2027  SpaceNews
  3. China advances efforts for lunar exploration, crewed moon landing  news.cgtn.com
  4. Chinese astronauts in Moon  Arab News
  5. China eyes 2030 lunar landing  China Daily - Global Edition

item.title: Voyager 1 is now so far from Earth that a signal traveling at the speed of light takes more than 22 hours to reach it — so when engineers send a command, they can wait nearly two days to know whether the spacecraft responded - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Voyager 1 is now so far from Earth that a signal traveling at the speed of light takes more than 22 hours to reach it — so when engineers send a command, they can wait nearly two days to know whether the spacecraft responded  Space Daily
  2. NASA's Voyager 1 probe is about to reach a distance of 1 light-day from Earth. Here's why this cosmic milestone is so huge  BBC Sky at Night Magazine
  3. When will the Voyager spacecraft cease operations, and is there any possibility of their preservation?  Universe Space Tech
  4. Voyager 1's signal now takes more than 23 hours to reach Earth, and by the time NASA receives the next status check the spacecraft will already be 1.5 million kilometres further into interstellar space  Space Daily

item.title: Consciousness might not be something the brain creates — it might be a fundamental feature of reality itself, more like gravity than like a thought — and one of the most credentialed neuroscientists alive is now arguing that mainstream science has been wrong - Space Daily
item.description: Consciousness might not be something the brain creates — it might be a fundamental feature of reality itself, more like gravity than like a thought — and one of the most credentialed neuroscientists alive is now arguing that mainstream science has been wrong  Space DailySee more headlines & perspectives on Google News

item.title: Evidence of Ancient Life Found Buried Under an Asteroid Crater - ScienceAlert
item.description:
1. Evidence of Ancient Life Found Buried Under an Asteroid Crater  ScienceAlert
  2. Scientists Just Found Evidence That Asteroids May Have Helped Create Life on Earth  SciTechDaily
  3. Life on Earth may have originated in the lakes remaining after meteorite impacts  Universe Space Tech
  4. How Asteroid Strikes May Have Sparked Earth’s First Breath of Oxygen  ScienceBlog.com
  5. Evidence of ancient life has just been found buried inside an asteroid crater — and the discovery suggests the warm lakes created by major impacts may have been some of the original cradles of life on Earth  Space Daily

item.title: Heavily reddened quasars caught going through a 'blow-out' phase - Phys.org
item.description: Heavily reddened quasars caught going through a 'blow-out' phase  Phys.org

item.title: Titan’s atmosphere is thicker than Earth’s, its rivers and lakes are made of methane and ethane, and NASA is sending a nuclear-powered drone there because on Saturn’s largest moon, flying may be easier than driving. - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Titan’s atmosphere is thicker than Earth’s, its rivers and lakes are made of methane and ethane, and NASA is sending a nuclear-powered drone there because on Saturn’s largest moon, flying may be easier than driving.  Space Daily
  2. Titan’s Organic World  astrobiology.com
  3. Is Titan Bigger Than Earth?  WorldAtlas

item.title: In 1990, after years of lobbying by Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 turned its camera back toward home from about 6 billion kilometres away and photographed Earth as a pale blue speck smaller than a single pixel — an image NASA had repeatedly resisted because it - Space Daily
item.description: In 1990, after years of lobbying by Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 turned its camera back toward home from about 6 billion kilometres away and photographed Earth as a pale blue speck smaller than a single pixel — an image NASA had repeatedly resisted because it  Space Daily

item.title: New high-performance generator creates stable power from humidity - Interesting Engineering
item.description:
1. New high-performance generator creates stable power from humidity  Interesting Engineering
  2. Figure 1 (IMAGE)  EurekAlert!
  3. From moisture to electricity: Scientists show off how kitchen items can power wearables and smart home devices  Digital Trends
  4. It seems simple, but it generates electricity: scientists transform ambient humidity into electricity with gelatin and salt, and the generator still works for more than 30 days.  CPG Click Petróleo e Gás
  5. Scientists generate electricity from ambient moisture using everyday ingredients  Tech Xplore

item.title: In 1908 something exploded over a remote part of Siberia with the force of roughly 1,000 Hiroshima bombs, flattened 80 million trees, and was heard 600 miles away — and more than a century later, scientists still cannot fully agree on whether it was an asteroi - Space Daily
item.description:
1. In 1908 something exploded over a remote part of Siberia with the force of roughly 1,000 Hiroshima bombs, flattened 80 million trees, and was heard 600 miles away — and more than a century later, scientists still cannot fully agree on whether it was an asteroi  Space Daily
  2. In 1908, something exploded in the sky over Siberia with hundreds of times the energy of the Hiroshima bomb, flattening more than 2,000 square kilometres of forest. When scientists finally reached the site years later, they found no crater at all  Space Daily

item.title: China launches 'human artificial embryos' to space in bid to see whether reproduction is possible off-world - Live Science
item.description:
1. China launches 'human artificial embryos' to space in bid to see whether reproduction is possible off-world  Live Science
  2. Can humans reproduce in space? China sends artificial embryos into orbit to find out  ThePrint
  3. Never-before-experiment: China sends human embryos to space  madhyamamonline.com
  4. News China Has Sent Human Embryos Into Space – Artificial Ones, for Now  Asgardia - The Space Nation
  5. Chinese space station launches pioneering experiment on human reproduction in space  AKIpress News Agency

item.title: Chinese scientists suggest harsh environments foster early human creativity - South China Morning Post
item.description:
1. Chinese scientists suggest harsh environments foster early human creativity  South China Morning Post
  2. A Radical Innovation Helped Archaic Humans Survive a Harsh Ice Age  ScienceAlert
  3. Crystals inside an ancient bone in central China just overturned a 146,000-year-old story about humans  The Times of India
  4. 146,000-Year-Old Discovery Rewrites the Story of Human Creativity  SciTechDaily
  5. Human creativity may have been forged by hardship rather than abundance — according to a new study of 146,000-year-old stone tools found in central China and dated to one of the harshest Ice Ages of early human history  Space Daily

item.title: Every GPS satellite is launched with a clock deliberately set to run slow, because Einstein's relativity speeds it up by about 38 microseconds a day once in orbit — and without that built-in correction, your phone's location would drift by roughly ten kilometres a d - Space Daily
item.description: Every GPS satellite is launched with a clock deliberately set to run slow, because Einstein's relativity speeds it up by about 38 microseconds a day once in orbit — and without that built-in correction, your phone's location would drift by roughly ten kilometres a d  Space Daily

item.description: Something ‘unprecedented’ is now happening to Earth’s rotation, scientists say  BBC Science Focus Magazine

item.description:

item.title: New light-based switch could cut chip energy use and speed future AI photonics - Tech Xplore
item.description:
1. New light-based switch could cut chip energy use and speed future AI photonics  Tech Xplore
  2. Physicists create hybrid light-matter particles that interact strongly enough to compute  Phys.org
  3. Light-controlled switch uses only 4 femtojoules and brings photonic AI chips closer to a future with less heat and lower energy consumption  CPG Click Petróleo e Gás
  4. Forget electrons, this breakthrough uses light-matter particles to power AI  ScienceDaily
  5. Photonic computing component (IMAGE)  EurekAlert!

item.title: New 'AI scientists' are improving—but reveal their fundamental limits - Phys.org
item.description:
1. New 'AI scientists' are improving—but reveal their fundamental limits  Phys.org
  2. AI & Science: What Is the Future of Discovery?  American Academy of Arts and Sciences
  3. Are ‘AI co-scientist’ tools actually useful for scientists?  statnews.com
  4. AI co-scientist startup CTO on 'hypothesis slop,' deterministic code and the goal of compressing a century of science into a decade  R&D World
  5. Accelerating Discovery With AI | Newswise  Newswise

item.title: Tiny fossils found in 1.7-billion‑year‑old mud yield clues to the evolution of complex life - Phys.org
item.description:
1. Tiny fossils found in 1.7-billion‑year‑old mud yield clues to the evolution of complex life  Phys.org

  3. Scientists Just Found 12,000 Tiny Fossils That Expand Our Understanding Of The Origins Of Complex Life  IFLScience
  4. Evidence for oldest complex life unearthed in Australian rocks  SMH.com.au
  5. Early Complex Life Lived In Oxygenated Seas, Challenging Long‑held Views Of Evolution  astrobiology.com

  3. New issue: Inside a Black Hole  BBC Science Focus Magazine

item.description:
1. Doomsday Glacier Shows Signs of Imminent Disintegration  Futurism

  4. Map Shows US Cities That Could Go Underwater if Sea Levels Rise 3 Meters  Newsweek
  5. Antarctic glacier collapses at record speed as Hektoria retreats 15 miles in just 15 months  ScienceDaily

item.title: A NASA satellite launched in 1976 carries a Carl Sagan–designed plaque sealed inside its core, mapping Earth's continents 268 million years ago, at launch, and 8.4 million years from now — and that last date is no accident, because it's roughly when the sat - Space Daily
item.description: A NASA satellite launched in 1976 carries a Carl Sagan–designed plaque sealed inside its core, mapping Earth's continents 268 million years ago, at launch, and 8.4 million years from now — and that last date is no accident, because it's roughly when the sat  Space Daily

item.title: The first all-female spacewalk in history did not happen until October 2019, fifty-eight years after Yuri Gagarin orbited Earth — and an earlier all-female attempt seven months before was reassigned because only one medium spacesuit torso was ready on the s - Space Daily
item.description: The first all-female spacewalk in history did not happen until October 2019, fifty-eight years after Yuri Gagarin orbited Earth — and an earlier all-female attempt seven months before was reassigned because only one medium spacesuit torso was ready on the s  Space Daily

item.title: The Voyager Golden Record carries greetings in 55 languages — a deliberate attempt to send a small sample of human voices into deep space long after the spacecraft fell silent. - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The Voyager Golden Record carries greetings in 55 languages — a deliberate attempt to send a small sample of human voices into deep space long after the spacecraft fell silent.  Space Daily
  2. The Voyager Golden Record carries a small sample of uranium on its cover, placed there so that whoever finds it can measure the decay and work out how long it has been drifting — a built-in clock for a message engineered to last around a billion years.  Space Daily
  3. The Voyager probes carry golden records etched with greetings in 55 languages, whale songs, and a woman's brainwaves recorded while she thought about being in love, and the records are designed to remain playable for one billion years drifting through i  Space Daily

item.title: Controlled experiments reveal how nuclear fallout particles form - Phys.org
item.description: Controlled experiments reveal how nuclear fallout particles form  Phys.org

item.title: The Vela satellites were built to catch secret nuclear tests, but they accidentally recorded flashes from deep space that opened a new branch of astrophysics - Space Daily
item.description: The Vela satellites were built to catch secret nuclear tests, but they accidentally recorded flashes from deep space that opened a new branch of astrophysics  Space Daily

item.title: Atomic oxygen in low Earth orbit slowly eats spacecraft surfaces, and the ISS survives because engineers learned to coat, test, and replace the materials most vulnerable to it - Space Daily
item.description: Atomic oxygen in low Earth orbit slowly eats spacecraft surfaces, and the ISS survives because engineers learned to coat, test, and replace the materials most vulnerable to it  Space Daily

item.title: The Hubble Deep Field began as a gamble on a tiny patch of sky that had been chosen because it looked almost empty, and it ended by revealing nearly 3,000 galaxies hiding in what seemed like nothing. - Space Daily
item.description: The Hubble Deep Field began as a gamble on a tiny patch of sky that had been chosen because it looked almost empty, and it ended by revealing nearly 3,000 galaxies hiding in what seemed like nothing.  Space Daily

item.title: I spent years assuming my personality was fixed — then I learned what neuroplasticity actually means and realised I had been maintaining myself like a finished product instead of a living system - Space Daily
item.description: I spent years assuming my personality was fixed — then I learned what neuroplasticity actually means and realised I had been maintaining myself like a finished product instead of a living system  Space Daily
